title: ArrayList — dynamic arrays
instruction:
Unlike arrays, `ArrayList` grows automatically. It implements the `List` interface.

```java
List<String> names = new ArrayList<>();
names.add("Alice");
names.get(0);       // "Alice"
names.size();       // 1
```

Run this — build a list and manipulate it.
successMessage: ArrayList prints like [a, b, c]. Collections.sort() sorts in place. .contains() is O(n) — linear search. For fast contains checks, use HashSet instead.

title: HashMap — key-value pairs
instruction:
`HashMap<K, V>` maps keys to values with O(1) average lookup:

```java
Map<String, Integer> ages = new HashMap<>();
ages.put("Alice", 25);
ages.get("Alice");           // 25
ages.getOrDefault("Bob", 0); // 0 if key not found
```
hint: Iterate entries with: for (Map.Entry<String, Integer> entry : map.entrySet())
successMessage: getOrDefault prevents NullPointerException when a key is missing. entrySet() gives you both key and value per entry. HashMap order is not guaranteed — use LinkedHashMap for insertion order, TreeMap for sorted order.

title: HashSet — unique items only
instruction:
`HashSet<T>` stores unique items with O(1) average `contains()`.

Perfect for: deduplication, membership testing, set operations.

Remove duplicates from a list using a HashSet.
hint: new HashSet<>(list) deduplicates the list in one step.
successMessage: new HashSet<>(list) deduplicates in one step. retainAll = intersection. removeAll = difference. addAll = union. These set operations are used in data analysis and permission systems.

title: TreeMap — sorted keys
instruction:
`TreeMap` keeps keys sorted (ascending by default). Use it when iteration order matters.

Unlike HashMap (O(1)), TreeMap is O(log n) — worth it when you need sorted output.
successMessage: TreeMap output is alphabetically sorted — 'brown' before 'dog' before 'fox'. The frequency counter pattern (getOrDefault + put) is one of the most common interview and real-world patterns.

title: Queue and Deque
instruction:
`Queue` = FIFO (first in, first out). `Deque` = double-ended queue.

- `offer(item)` — add to tail
- `poll()` — remove from head (null if empty)
- `peek()` — view head without removing

Use `ArrayDeque` as the implementation — faster than LinkedList.
successMessage: Queue serves Alice first (FIFO). Stack pops 3 first (LIFO). Use ArrayDeque for both — it's faster than LinkedList and the recommended choice for Java stacks and queues.

title: Collections utility methods
instruction:
`java.util.Collections` has powerful utilities:

- `Collections.sort(list)` — sort
- `Collections.reverse(list)` — reverse in place
- `Collections.shuffle(list)` — randomise
- `Collections.min(list)` / `max(list)`
- `Collections.frequency(list, value)` — count occurrences
- `Collections.unmodifiableList(list)` — read-only view
successMessage: Collections has 30+ utility methods. These are the foundation — you'll use sort, min, max, and frequency constantly. All work with any Collection (List, Queue, etc.).

title: Build an inventory manager
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build an `InventoryManager` using collections:
- `Map<String, Integer> stock` — product name to quantity
- `addProduct(String name, int qty)` — adds or increases stock
- `sellProduct(String name, int qty)` — reduces stock, throws if insufficient
- `getLowStock(int threshold)` — returns `List<String>` of products with stock <= threshold
- `printAll()` — sorted alphabetically by product name

Test with at least 5 products.
hint: Use TreeMap for automatic alphabetical order. getLowStock uses entrySet() and returns a filtered list.

codeChecks.message: Use a Map<String, Integer> for stock.

successMessage: A real inventory system built entirely with Java collections. TreeMap keeps it sorted for free. getLowStock is O(n) — checking every product. Chapter 16 done!
